title: Alphabet Game
description: 2-3 players are given a scene suggestion. They must do a scene where they complete the alphabet. The first person to speak starts their sentence with A, the second person starts theirs with B, and so on until they reach Z.

title: Alter Ego
description: 2 players are given a scene suggestion. At any point in the scene, one may step forward for an aside, freezing the second player in place. They begin their short aside with, “What they don’t know…” and tell us all about their scheme. Both complete 1-2 asides, then scene ends.

title: Apocalypse
description: This long form takes place in 2 parts. Get cause of apocalypse from audience suggestion. Part 1 is the eventé, or the scenes leading up to the end of the world. Part 2 is the monoscene, which takes place some time after the apocalypse. This may be a dystopian or post-apocalyptic world, where everything is turned upside down in a comedic way.

title: Bartender
description: One player is the bartender, and the other is a patron with a problem. The patron explains their problem in song, and the bartender responds with advice in song.

title: Celebrity Funeral
description: One person is the director of a funeral. They leave the room, and a mystery celebrity is decided on. Other players are friends/fam of the deceased who give guest speeches. Funeral director must guess what celebrity/character has died.

title: Crabtree’s Conundrum
description: Two players perform a scene. At any point, one may say 'what did you say?' and the other player must come up with something that responds with the last thing they said.

title: Dating Game
description: One player is an eligible bachelor/bachelorette, and three are contestants with secret traits given by the audience. The bachelor asks questions and must guess each contestant’s quirk.

title: Day in the Life
description: Improvisers interview an audience member about their day, then get an audience suggestion. This long form game recreates a day in the life of that audience member in ~30 minutes. Done best when one player takes on the role of the audience member, and other players act as people they interact with throughout their day.

title: Emotional Triad
description: The stage is divided into three emotional zones. After getting a scene suggestion, players must adopt the emotion of whichever zone they are in during the scene.

title: Emo Waiter
description: 2 players are out to dinner at a restaurant. They leave the room, and the audience decides an emotion for each. The 3rd player is the waiter, and interacts with each patron by reflecting their assigned emotion. Patron must guess what emotion they’ve been assigned.

title: Expert Opinion
description: A public hearing is held about a problem. Each player is an expert and must answer questions from others attending.

title: Film Critics
description: 2 players are actors in a movie based on a suggestion, and 2 players are film critics. Movie starts with an opening scene between actors, then is paused by film critics. They discuss the scene, decide to fast forward, and give a hint at what happens at the climax. Actors perform the scene based on the hint. Movie is paused, film critics explain what will happen at the end, and fast forward to it. Actors perform it based on the hint.

title: Film Noir
description: 2 Players sit on chairs facing the audience. Get a location suggestion from the audience, and one player begins describing the scene in intense detail. After about 30 seconds, switch to the second player, who describes the scene in intense detail from their character’s perspective. After both have spoken 2-3 times, the other players get up and act out the scene as vividly as possible, portraying everything they described at the beginning. In the acting phase, nothing can be added or changed, nor can dialogue be used unless it was explicitly stated what lines of dialogue were said in the beginning.

title: Foreign Film Dub
description: Two players act silently in a foreign film while two others provide improvised dubbed dialogue.

title: Funeral
description:
Get suggestion from audience: a weird way to die. With this suggestion to work from, the first scene performed is one where someone dies in that peculiar manner. Once someone has died, the scene is wiped, and flashes forward to a funeral service. The scene is set: the deceased player center stage, lying in a “coffin”. The other players are on the back line as attendees. They take turns stepping up to the podium to begin an anecdote about the deceased character. Once they have given a short intro to the story, the scene is wiped, and is then played out live. After the scene concludes, players go back to the funeral setup, first speaker leaves, and a new one comes forward to begin an anecdote. 

Death intro scene (5ish minutes, serves as a jumping off point) 
Funeral - first speaker intro their story (2ish minutes) 
Players act out first speaker’s story. The more actors, the better. 
Funeral - second speaker intro their story (2ish minutes) 
So on and so forth

title: Gaston
description:
Split players into 2 teams of 3. Choose a 1 syllable word such as “sit”, and begin cadence. Song follows cadence of “Gaston” from Beauty and the Beast. 

No one sits like Gaston
No one hits like Gaston
On the dance floor no one does the splits like Gaston
He is a fantastic gymnast!
     All: My what a guy that Gaston!

title: The Good, The Bad, and The Ugly
description: 3 players are on a talk show. Each is assigned to Good, Bad, or Dirty. Audience asks advice from each on problems occurring in their lives, and players must give a good response, a bad response, or a dirty response.

title: Good Cop, Bad Cop
description: 1 player is under interrogation for a wacky crime. 2 other players are Good Cop and Bad Cop, and must drop hints as to what crime player 1 committed while they interrogate them.

title: Half Life
description: Players perform a 1 minute scene. At the end, they replay the scene in 30 seconds. Then 15, then 7, then 3.

title: Helping Hands
description: One player has come to talk to their boss about a problem in the workplace. The boss is seated at a desk with another player behind them, acting as the boss’ arms. The scene continues with the employee trying to act casual as their boss is flailing around.

title: La Ronde
description:
This game works as a circle of 2 person scenes. In this game, each person only plays one character. Each scene is edited by one character replacing another. The person left on stage remains the same character, and the new player chooses a character, location, and situation (similar to a roundtable version of freeze). Done best when scenes can come together to make a coherent story. 

Start
Scene 1: Player 1 and 2 
Scene 2: Player 2 and 3
Scene 3: Player 3 and 4
Scene 4: Player 4 and 5
Scene 5: Player 5 and 6 
Scene 6: Player 6 and 7 
Scene 7: Player 7 and 1 
End

title: Letter to A
description: Players write a letter one word at a time, starting with 'Dear...' and ending with 'Sincerely, me.'

title: Lines From A
description: Players perform a scene based on an audience suggestion. There is a hat full of pre-written lines in the playing area, and at any time a player can take one to use, reading the line and continuing the scene with whatever was written.

title: Let Me Think About That
description: Two players perform a scene based on a suggestion. At any time, one player may say “let me think about that…” and the third player enters the scene and delivers a short monologue of what that player is thinking at the moment.

title: Montage
description:
A series of short, discordant scenes performed by all players. Can be done in sideline or back line format. A suggestion is given by the audience, and improvisers take turns jumping forward to perform a short interaction. 

Edits
Swipe edits: someone runs across front of playing space, ending scene and sending players back to line.
Freeze: someone calls freeze, then takes the place of one improviser, starting a new scene.
Director’s Cut: someone calls “cut”, then changes the scene by giving improvisers suggestions for improvement. 
New choice

title: Monologue
description:
Standard long form game, but players are not restricted to only one player. The story is told in 3 monologues separated by several scenes. Best when each monologue is done by a different improviser. 

Beginning Monologue (1-2 minutes)
Rising Action (10-15 minutes) 
Middle Monologue (1-2 minutes)
Falling Action/Concluding Scenes (10-15 minutes) 
Ending Monologue (1-2 minutes)

title: New Choice
description: Players perform a scene based on a suggestion. At any point, the fronter may call “new choice” and a player changes what they just did until fronter is satisfied.

title: No Minor Characters
description: Long-form game in which each improviser chooses one character, and must play them the whole time. Best practice for this style of game is to follow the “hero’s journey” format. A character exiting signifies a scene change for this game.

title: Oracle
description: Players join arms and act as the all knowing oracle. Speaking one word at a time, this oracle answers audience questions with their divine knowledge. When The Oracle is finished answering, they say (one word at a time) "The Oracle has spoken”

title: Oscar On
description: Players perform a scene based on a suggestion. At any point, fronter may tell a player, “Oscar On”. That player steps forward and delivers an Oscar winning monologue. When fronter calls “Oscar Off”, the scene continues as normal.

title: Park Bench
description: All players line up. One player begins on a bench, and another sits down to perform a short, 1 minute scene interacting with them. After the scene is concluded, the starter exits, the second player moves over, and a new player enters the scene, so on and so forth until everyone has gone.

title: Pan Four
description: 4 players stand as the corners of a large square. Based on fronter’s direction, the square can rotate left, right, uh X (across the middle). Choose a suggestion for each possible group of 2 improvisers. Scenes rotate as fronter calls them to change.

title: Party Quirks
description: One player is hosting a party. All other players are given traits, characters, or quirks to act as. The party host must welcome attendees to their party and interact with them to gain hints. As host guesses each attendee’s quirk, they exit the party until the host is alone again.

title: Pavlov’s Response
description: Each player is given a trigger word, such as “coffee”. A scene takes place. Every time a player’s trigger word is spoken, they must exit or enter the scene.

title: Pillars
description: 2 players are performing a short scene. Assign the other players to each, and at any point an actor may clap, and their assistant has to make a sound effect or say a word that will be used to continue the scene.

title: POW
description: Played like a game of telephone, only the starter knows the clues. Audience gives them a place, and occupation, and a weapon. One by one, the next player comes in and watches the starter mime the clues. When they think they know one, they clap. When they think they know the weapon, audience says, “3,2,1, kill!” and they kill the starter with the weapon. Then the next player comes in, so on and so forth. At the end of the game, each says what they thought the clues were.

title: Queen is Coming
description: 2 players are performing a scene based on audience suggestion. At some point, fronter calls “queen is coming”, and the boss of the 2 players enters, asks what they think they’re doing, and gives them new orders. Players have to act casual and try not to get in trouble with boss. Boss enters scene 3 times before it ends.

title: Questions Only
description: 2 person scene based on a suggestion where the only lines allowed to be spoken between the two are questions.

title: Replay
description: Players perform a short scene (1-2 minutes). At the end, audience gives a suggestion for how to replay it. Some examples of replay suggestions are: changing emotion, changing speed, changing accent, changing time period, etc.

title: Ring Ring
description: 2 players are performing a scene, and 2 players are waiting to call them. Anytime one of the callers says 'Ring Ring', that player must find an excuse to get on the phone, regardless of what is happening in the scene at that time.

title: Roll For It
description: 2 players perform a short scene while one sits aside with a d20. A certain amount of times chosen beforehand the player with the dice can call to roll for it when they see or hear an action, in which they roll the dice. The player will preform the action on a scale of 1-20.

title: Scene in the Dark
description: Lights are turned off, and 2 players perform a complete, verbal only scene. Use sound effects to your advantage.

title: Slam Poetry
description: Players form a backline, and audience gives each a suggestion for an emotion. Get a suggestion for a starting line, and players must step forward and create a spontaneous poem starting with the line, and including the emotion they were given.

title: Shoehorn
description: NEEDS FURTHER DEVELOPMENT

title: Silence
description: 2 person scene in which the fronter can call silence at any point. If silence is called, players must continue the scene nonverbally until silence is lifted by the fronter.

title: Sit, Stand, Lean
description: 3 person scene in which one player must be standing, one sitting, and one leaning at all times. Players must pay attention to each other and pick up whichever position isn’t occupied. Use 2 chairs for scene.

title: Sounds Like a Song Cue
description: At any point in the scene, one player may say “that sounds like a song cue” to the other. That player must then perform an improvised song based on the line they were prompted for. After one player performs a song, the other player performs a song when prompted.

title: Space Jump
description: One player begins moving on stage, and a second player calls “space jump!”, causing the first player to freeze in place. The second player then enters the stage and starts a new scene based on the pose. At some point, a third player calls “space jump”, and both players freeze. They enter and start a new scene. Repeat with the fourth player. Players exit scene in reverse order, and the original scenes resume as people leave.

title: Superheroes
description: Improvisers are given a suggestion for a world-altering situation and a superhero that doesn’t exist. The first scene begins with that character trying to solve the problem, realizing they can’t, and calling another superhero by name to help. (Ex: Doesn’t Wear Pants Man, etc). The new superhero joins the scene, then calls another hero. So on and so forth until everyone is in the scene, then they leave in the opposite order they entered.

title: Taxi
description: 4 chairs are set up in a square. Each player is given a character or trait, and enter the taxi one by one. Each time a new person enters, all players adopt that trait. They exit in the reverse order.

title: Television
description: Players are given a suggestion, and members of the audience have the ability to change the scene like they're using a remote

title: Three Rooms
description: 3 separate two person scenes are taking place in the performing area. Each scene is given a room and a suggestion, and fronter calls to switch between rooms. When scene switches, the new room must start their scene with the last line of the previous room.

title: Town Hall
description: A new law has been passed in the town, and all improvisers are concerned citizens. Fronter points to switch between all characters. When a character is pointed at, they step forward, introduce themself, and begin monologuing about their opinion on the issue.

title: True Hollywood Stories
description:
This longform follows the format of an Entertainment Network celebrity exposé. Begin by interviewing an audience member. Make sure they are comfortable answering questions and being the subject of a long form. 

Info needed from audience member: 
Basic upbringing: family size, hometown, anything specific and interesting childhood events
What did they always want to be when they grew up? This is important because it’s what we’re going to make them famous for. What interested them in it? 
One thing they got in trouble for, from going to jail to getting their phone taken for staying up too late. It doesn’t matter how major of a thing it was, because we’re going to make it a big thing. This will be their tragic downfall later. 
Once the interviewee leaves the stage, the scene begins. It opens with everyone saying, “We now present: The True Hollywood Story of _____”. The opening scenes are a combination of short narrations and talking heads clips. One player takes a seat and begins acting as someone who knew the subject personally. Similar to a documentary, they should identify themselves and say how they knew the main character, then tell an anecdote about the character. This kind of segment is called a “Talking Head”. Players take turns giving statements, tagging each other out faster and faster until it is a flurry of talking. 

Then, a narrator gives us an intro, such as “John Doe was fairly standard in his small town growing up. It wasn’t until he won the Zach and Cody Danimals Sweepstakes that he begin to rise to fame as a playground bully. This behavior continued for much of his early life, until he found himself the new kid at an NYC law firm, where he was suddenly the small fish in a big pond…” 

After the intro, scenes begin. A typical format may look like: 

Interview with audience member (3-5 minutes) 
Talking heads, narration opening (2-3 minutes) 
Humble beginnings scene (5-7 minutes) 
Narration (1 minute) 
Rise to fame scene (5-7 minutes) 
Narration (1 minute) 
Tragic downfall scene (7 minutes) 
Concluding narration (1 minute)

title: Typewriter
description: Get a suggestion for a book title that doesn’t exist. One player will be the author of the book, and we get to see them writing the first chapter. Author will sit in a chair and narrate the events of the book while other players act out what is happening behind them.

title: Warp To
description:
Players get a suggestion for a scene, and begin it. At any point, the fronter may call “warp to…” any time frame, event, or interaction mentioned in the scene, and the scene switches to that scene. 

This reminds me of back in third grade when we had a lemonade stand. 
I remember, a raccoon came by and stole all our money! 
Fronter: Warp to the lemonade stand in third grade.

title: Weekend At Bernie's
description: Players do a short scene based on a suggestion. At the end of the scene, the audience votes by applause on which player they want to “kill”. That person is now dead, and the scene must repeat, with the other players puppeting arms and legs. This continues until there is one person left standing.

title: Wikipedia
description: Audience gives a suggestion for a Wikipedia article that doesn’t exist, and fronter sets timer for 4-5 minutes. Players form a backline, and a fronter points at one to start. They must narrate typing this Wikipedia article, being careful to avoid using opinions, stuttering, or hesitating. If someone messes up, another player can call “objection!” and explain why they messed up. Fronter either sustains or overrules the objection. If objection is overruled, original player continues writing. If objection is sustained, the objecting player steps up to continue the article. Play scene until timer runs out.
